title: Advanced Graph Algorithms in Java
summary: Dijkstra's shortest path, topological sort (Kahn's BFS + DFS), Bellman-Ford, and Minimum Spanning Tree (Prim/Kruskal).

questions.prompt: Dijkstra's algorithm finds:
choices: Shortest paths from one source to all vertices in a weighted graph
questions.explanation: Single-source shortest path on graphs with non-negative edge weights.

questions.prompt: Dijkstra fails with negative edge weights because:
choices: The heap can't store negative numbers
Once a node is settled its distance is considered final — a later negative edge could improve it
questions.explanation: The greedy invariant breaks: a 'settled' node could be reached more cheaply via a not-yet-settled node + negative edge.

questions.prompt: Dijkstra time complexity with a binary min-heap:
questions.explanation: Each vertex is extracted once O(V log V) and each edge may trigger a heap update O(E log V).

questions.prompt: Dijkstra from node 0: edges 0→1(4), 0→2(1), 2→1(2). Shortest dist to node 1:
questions.explanation: Path 0→2→1 costs 1+2=3 < direct edge 4. Dijkstra finds 3.

questions.prompt: The 'stale entry skip' check (if d > dist[u] continue) in Dijkstra is needed because:
choices: A node can be re-added to the heap with a better distance; old entries become outdated
questions.explanation: When a shorter path is found, the old heap entry is not removed — it's simply ignored when polled.

questions.prompt: Topological sort is only valid on:
questions.explanation: A cycle means there is no valid ordering (A before B before A is impossible).

questions.prompt: Kahn's algorithm starts by enqueuing nodes with:
questions.explanation: Nodes with no incoming edges have no dependencies — they can be processed first.

questions.prompt: If Kahn's algorithm finishes with output size < n, it means:
choices: A cycle exists in the graph
questions.explanation: Cyclic nodes can never reach in-degree 0 — they are never enqueued.

questions.prompt: Topological sort of: A→C, B→C, C→D. One valid order:
questions.explanation: A and B (in-degree 0) come first, then C (after both done), then D. A B C D is valid.

questions.prompt: DFS topological sort pushes a node to the result stack:
choices: After ALL its descendants are fully processed (post-order)
questions.explanation: Post-order ensures all nodes that depend on u are already in the stack before u is pushed.

questions.prompt: Bellman-Ford runs how many edge-relaxation passes?
questions.explanation: Shortest paths use at most V-1 edges. One pass extends paths by one hop, so V-1 passes cover all possible paths.

questions.prompt: Bellman-Ford detects negative cycles by:
choices: Running a V-th relaxation pass — if any distance still improves, a negative cycle exists
questions.explanation: After V-1 passes all shortest paths are final. Improvement in pass V means a negative cycle allows infinite cost reduction.

questions.prompt: Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford — when to use which:
choices: Dijkstra for non-negative weights (faster); Bellman-Ford when negative weights exist
questions.explanation: Dijkstra O((V+E)log V) beats Bellman-Ford O(VE) but requires non-negative weights.

questions.prompt: A Minimum Spanning Tree of a graph with V vertices has exactly:
questions.explanation: A spanning tree on V nodes always has exactly V-1 edges — minimum to stay connected without a cycle.

questions.prompt: Kruskal's MST algorithm adds edges in order of:
choices: Increasing weight — greedily pick cheapest that don't form a cycle
questions.explanation: Sort edges ascending, add each if it connects two different components (Union-Find check).

questions.prompt: Prim's MST algorithm is most similar to:
choices: Dijkstra — both grow a 'settled' set using a min-heap on edge costs
questions.explanation: Both use a min-heap to greedily pick the cheapest next step; Dijkstra minimises cumulative path, Prim minimises single edge.

questions.prompt: Union-Find is used in Kruskal's because:
choices: It detects whether adding an edge creates a cycle in O(α(n)) time
questions.explanation: Two nodes in the same component means adding an edge between them creates a cycle.

questions.prompt: Which graph problems require a min-heap for efficient solution?
questions.explanation: Kahn's uses a plain queue (BFS on in-degrees). Bellman-Ford iterates all edges — no heap needed.

questions.prompt: Course schedule problem (can all courses be completed given prerequisites?) maps to:
choices: Topological sort — cycle detection on a DAG
questions.explanation: Prerequisites are directed edges. If the dependency graph has a cycle, not all courses can be taken.

questions.prompt: Floyd-Warshall finds:
questions.explanation: DP on all (source, destination) pairs. Works with negative weights (not negative cycles). O(V³) time O(V²) space.

flashcards.back: Single-source shortest path. Min-heap. O((V+E)log V). Non-negative weights only.
if (d > dist[u]) continue — ignore outdated heap entries.
Order nodes so every edge u→v has u before v. Only on DAGs.
BFS on in-degrees. Enqueue in-degree-0 nodes. Output size < n → cycle.
V-1 relaxation passes. Handles negative weights. V-th pass detects negative cycle.
V-1 edges connecting all V nodes with minimum total weight, no cycles.
Sort edges by weight, add if no cycle (Union-Find check). O(E log E).
Grow tree from one node via min-heap on edge costs. Like Dijkstra for MST.

project.brief:
Write AdvancedGraphsLab.java:
1. dijkstra(List<List<int[]>> adj, int src, int n) → int[]
2. topoSortKahn(List<List<Integer>> adj, int n) → List<Integer>  (empty list if cycle)
3. canFinish(int numCourses, int[][] prerequisites) → boolean  (course schedule)
4. bellmanFord(int n, int[][] edges, int src) → int[]  (null if negative cycle)
Include main() tests: graph with shorter indirect path, DAG, cyclic graph, graph with negative edge.

checklist.text: topoSortKahn returns empty list when cycle detected (output.size() < n)
canFinish reuses Kahn's cycle detection logic
bellmanFord returns null on negative cycle detection in V-th pass

hints: dijkstra: adj entries are int[]{neighbour, weight}. Initialise all dist[] to Integer.MAX_VALUE.
topoSortKahn: build inDegree[] by scanning all adjacency lists before starting BFS.
bellmanFord: guard against Integer.MAX_VALUE + weight overflow with a != MAX_VALUE check.
